mnemonics.title: NOT Gate - The Inverter
mnemonics.content: NOT flips the bit: 0 becomes 1, 1 becomes 0. Think of it as a 'opposite day' gate.

mnemonics.title: AND Gate - 'All Must Be 1'
mnemonics.content: AND = 'All Need 1'. Output is 1 ONLY when ALL inputs are 1. Remember: 'A for All'

mnemonics.title: OR Gate - 'Any Will Do'
mnemonics.content: OR = 'One or the other or both'. Output is 1 if AT LEAST ONE input is 1. Remember: 'O for OR, output is 1 for mOre than zero'

mnemonics.title: NAND Gate - 'Not AND'
mnemonics.content: NAND is the opposite of AND. It's AND followed by NOT. Think: 'Not All' - output is 0 only when all inputs are 1.

mnemonics.title: NOR Gate - 'Not OR'
mnemonics.content: NOR is the opposite of OR. It's OR followed by NOT. Think: 'None of them' - output is 1 only when ALL inputs are 0.

mnemonics.title: XOR Gate - 'Exclusive OR'
mnemonics.content: XOR = 'One or the other, but NOT both'. Output is 1 when inputs are DIFFERENT. Remember: 'X' means 'different/unique'

mnemonics.title: XNOR Gate - 'Not XOR'
mnemonics.content: XNOR = 'Not Exclusive OR'. Output is 1 when inputs are SAME (both 0 or both 1). Think: 'X' with 'NO' - no difference means 1.

acronyms.usage: Use when you need to invert or complement a signal. Output is the opposite of input.
Use when output depends on ALL inputs being true (1). Common in conditions where all requirements must be met.
Use when output depends on ANY input being true (1). Common in conditions where meeting any requirement suffices.
Use as a universal gate - can create any other gate from NAND gates. Output is 0 only when all inputs are 1.
Use as another universal gate. Output is 1 only when all inputs are 0.
Use for parity checking, addition (half adder), and comparing bits. Output 1 when inputs differ.
Use for equality comparison. Output 1 when inputs are same. Also called 'coincidence gate'.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a bubble (circle) at the output - this 'bubble' represents inversion. The triangle points right, bubble negates.
The AND symbol looks like a D shape (flat back, curved front). Think: 'D' stands for 'Doin' nothing until all inputs arrive.
The OR symbol has a curved back (like a shield). Think: 'OR' opens the door with just one key.
XOR has an extra curved line at the input. The 'X' represents 'cross-over' or difference between inputs.
For 2 inputs: 00→0, 01→1, 10→1, 11→0. Notice the pattern: 0,1,1,0 - like counting in binary (0,1,2,3) but checking odd parity.
NAND and NOR are universal because they can build all other gates. Draw them as 'starting blocks' in gate design.

analogies.analogy: Think of a light switch with a NOT gate: press 'off' (0), light turns 'on' (1). It's like a reflection in a mirror - up becomes down.
Think of a bank locker requiring TWO keys to open. Both keys (inputs) must be present (1) to get output 1. Missing any key means no access.
Think of a door with two doorbells. If EITHER bell is pressed, the chime sounds. Even if both are pressed, it still sounds.
Think of a 'NOT ALL' scenario: A team wins (output 1) unless ALL players perform perfectly. Only when everyone fails does the team fail.
Think of a 'NONE' situation: A fire alarm sounds (1) only when NONE of the sensors detect fire (all 0). If any sensor detects, alarm stays silent.
Think of a light controlled by two switches at different ends of a room. If switches are DIFFERENT (one up, one down), light is ON. If SAME (both up or both down), light is OFF.
Think of married couple decision-making: They agree (output 1) when they have the SAME opinion (both say yes or both say no). They disagree when opinions differ.
